bytes.topic: IaC Philosophy — reproducibility, drift detection, auditability, why IaC matters
bytes.title: Bicep IaC Philosophy — Reproducibility, Drift, and Why It Matters
bytes.body:
Bicep enforces a declarative contract between your intent and your Azure environment. Every `.bicep` file compiles to an ARM template and is submitted via `az deployment group create` — Azure's Resource Manager reconciles declared state against live state on every run. This idempotency is the foundation of reproducibility: the same template deployed ten times produces the same resources.

Drift — when live resources diverge from declared state — is Bicep's silent enemy. Engineers who click through the portal to "just fix something quickly" break the contract. Detect drift by running `az deployment group what-if` in CI: it diffs the compiled ARM template against the actual resource group state and surfaces unexpected changes before they become incidents.

Auditability comes from two layers: Bicep source in Git (who changed what param, when, and why) and ARM deployment history in Azure (`az deployment group list`), which stores every submitted template with its timestamp and principal. Together they give you a complete chain of custody — mandatory for SOC 2 and ISO 27001 audits.

Treat your Bicep files as the single source of truth. If it's not in the repo, it doesn't exist.
bytes.code_snippet:
// what-if in CI — detect drift before apply
az deployment group what-if \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @prod.bicepparam \
  --result-format FullResourcePayloads

// audit: list last 10 deployments with initiator
az deployment group list \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "[].{name:name, state:properties.provisioningState, ts:properties.timestamp, by:properties.outputResources[0].id}" \
  --top 10 \
  --output table

bytes.topic: Terraform Fundamentals — providers, resources, state, plan/apply lifecycle
bytes.title: Bicep vs Terraform: Providers, State & Plan/Apply — What Bicep Does Differently
bytes.body:
Terraform's provider/state/plan-apply model has direct analogues in Bicep, but the mechanics differ significantly. In Bicep, providers aren't declared separately — they're embedded in the resource type string itself (e.g., `Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks@2023-04-01`). The `@apiVersion` suffix pins the provider contract, equivalent to locking a Terraform provider version in `required_providers`.

State is the biggest conceptual shift. Bicep has no local `.tfstate` file. Azure Resource Manager owns the truth — every deployment is a declarative diff against ARM's server-side state. This eliminates state drift and backend locking nightmares, but you lose Terraform's fine-grained `import` and `state mv` primitives.

The plan/apply lifecycle maps cleanly: `az deployment group what-if` surfaces a change preview (create, modify, delete, no-change) before you commit. `az deployment group create` applies it. The `--confirm-with-what-if` flag fuses both steps into a single interactive command. `--mode Complete` is the nuclear option — resources absent from the template are deleted, matching Terraform's full `destroy`-then-`apply` sweep.

bytes.topic: State Management — remote state, state locking, backend configuration
bytes.title: Bicep State Management — Deployment Stacks Are Your terraform.tfstate
bytes.body:
Bicep doesn't use a state file — Azure Resource Manager is the backend. ARM tracks deployed resource properties natively, so every `az deployment group create` compares your template against live resource state. But for lifecycle control over orphaned resources and drift prevention, Deployment Stacks are Bicep's answer to Terraform-style state management.

A stack groups resources under a single tracked unit scoped to a resource group or subscription. When you remove a resource from your Bicep file and redeploy the stack, `--action-on-unmanage deleteResources` instructs ARM to delete the detached resource automatically — no manual cleanup, no orphan sprawl.

For locking, stacks support `--deny-settings-mode denyWritesAndDeletes` at the stack level, blocking out-of-band writes without scattering `Microsoft.Authorization/locks` resources across templates. `--deny-settings-apply-to-child-scopes` extends protection to nested resource groups.

Stack state lives entirely in ARM — query it with `az stack group show` to inspect managed resources and their `resourcesCleanupAction` status. This gives you a deterministic, auditable deployment lifecycle with zero external state backend to configure, secure, or version.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Create/update a Deployment Stack — Bicep's stateful deployment primitive
az stack group create \
  --name "prod-infra-stack" \
  --resource-group "rg-prod" \
  --template-file "./main.bicep" \
  --parameters ./main.bicepparam \
  --action-on-unmanage deleteResources \
  --deny-settings-mode denyWritesAndDeletes \
  --deny-settings-apply-to-child-scopes \
  --yes

# Inspect managed resource state and cleanup assignments
az stack group show \
  --name "prod-infra-stack" \
  --resource-group "rg-prod" \
  --query "resources[].{id:id,action:resourcesCleanupAction}"

bytes.topic: Modules — reusable modules, registry, versioning strategy
bytes.title: Bicep Modules — Registry Publishing and Versioning Strategy
bytes.body:
Bicep modules let you encapsulate resource definitions into reusable units, but the real power kicks in when you publish them to a private Azure Container Registry (ACR). Use `az bicep publish` to push a module to your registry under a semantic version tag. Consumers reference it via the `br:` scheme with a pinned version — never a mutable `latest` tag in production.

Version your modules with `major.minor.patch` semantics: bump major for breaking parameter changes, minor for new optional parameters, patch for bug fixes or metadata tweaks. Store the module source in a `modules/` directory at the repo root and gate publish to the registry behind a CI step that enforces version bumps before merge.

For team-wide adoption, set a `bicepconfig.json` at the repo root with a `moduleAliases` block — this lets consumers write `br/shared:storage/v2` instead of the full ACR URL, making module references refactor-safe if you ever migrate registries.

bytes.topic: Workspaces & Environments — managing dev/staging/prod with the same codebase
bytes.title: Bicep Workspaces — One Codebase, Three Environments
bytes.body:
Bicep has no native 'workspace' concept, but parameter files give you the same isolation. Create a `main.bicep` with parameterized resource names, SKUs, and replica counts, then pair it with `dev.bicepparam`, `staging.bicepparam`, and `prod.bicepparam`. Each `.bicepparam` file uses the `using` keyword to bind to the same template, so drift between environments is structurally impossible — you can't accidentally deploy a prod SKU to dev when the SKU is a parameter.

Use `targetScope = 'subscription'` at the top of your main template so all three environments deploy into separate resource groups (`rg-myapp-dev`, `rg-myapp-staging`, `rg-myapp-prod`). Reference the param files directly in your GitHub Actions workflow with `az deployment sub create --parameters dev.bicepparam`. For secrets, never hardcode them in param files — use `getSecret()` inside `.bicepparam` to pull values from a per-environment Key Vault at deploy time. This keeps all environment logic in the param layer and the template itself purely structural.
bytes.code_snippet:
// prod.bicepparam
using './main.bicep'

param environmentName = 'prod'
param location = 'eastus'
param appServiceSkuName = 'P2v3'
param minInstanceCount = 3
param keyVaultName = 'kv-myapp-prod'

param sqlAdminPassword = getSecret(
  '11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111',
  'rg-myapp-prod',
  'kv-myapp-prod',
  'sql-admin-password'
)

bytes.topic: Bicep — ARM template replacement, Azure-native, modules and parameters
bytes.title: Bicep — Azure's ARM Replacement with Modules and Typed Parameters
bytes.body:
Bicep compiles down to ARM JSON but writes like a real language. Every `.bicep` file is a first-class ARM template — no state files, no provider plugins, just `az deployment group create`. Parameters are strongly typed: `param sku string = 'Standard_LRS'` enforces type at compile time, and `@allowed(['Standard_LRS', 'Premium_LRS'])` adds ARM-level validation without custom policies.

Modules are the real unlock. A `module storageModule './storage.bicep' = { ... }` call compiles each module into a nested ARM deployment, preserving dependency tracking via `dependsOn` or implicit output references. Outputs wire modules together: `output storageId string = storageModule.outputs.id` lets a downstream module consume the resource without hardcoded IDs.

Scoping is explicit — `targetScope = 'subscription'` at the file top shifts deployment from resource group to subscription level, enabling management-group-wide policy assignments or role definitions in the same pipeline. Bicep's `existing` keyword references pre-existing resources (`resource vnet 'Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks@2023-04-01' existing = { name: vnetName }`) without importing them into state, avoiding accidental deletes.

bytes.topic: Pulumi — real programming languages for IaC, type safety, testing support
bytes.title: Bicep vs Pulumi — Real Languages, Type Safety, and Unit Testing You Actually Can't Do in Bicep
bytes.body:
Bicep's declarative DSL is expressive for Azure-native resources but hits a wall when logic gets complex. `for` loops require array comprehensions, conditionals are ternary-only, and there's no unit test framework — only `az deployment what-if` against live ARM. Pulumi flips this: you write real TypeScript, Python, Go, or C#, so conditionals, loops, and abstractions are first-class language constructs.

Where Bicep infers resource types from ARM schemas at author time via VS Code extension, Pulumi's `azure-native` TypeScript SDK ships full type definitions compiled from ARM specs. A misspelled property is a compile error, not a failed deployment.

Testing is where the gap is sharpest. Bicep has no unit test framework. Pulumi exposes `pulumi.runtime.setMocks()` — mock the resource graph, write Jest assertions, and validate policy compliance without touching Azure. Assert that `allowBlobPublicAccess` is `false` in CI before a single resource is provisioned.

The trade-off: Pulumi's state backend (Pulumi Cloud or self-hosted) adds operational overhead that Bicep sidesteps entirely by delegating state to ARM. For Azure-only teams with simple logic, Bicep wins on friction. Pulumi wins when your IaC needs conditionals, loops, reusable components, or testable correctness guarantees.

bytes.topic: AWS CDK — constructs, stacks, L1/L2/L3 abstraction levels
bytes.title: Bicep Dev's Guide to AWS CDK: Constructs, Stacks, and L1/L2/L3 Abstractions
bytes.body:
Bicep developers think in `resource` declarations and modules. AWS CDK maps to this mental model through a three-tier Construct hierarchy.

L1 Constructs (`CfnBucket`, `CfnFunction`) are 1:1 CloudFormation resource mappings — the equivalent of a raw Bicep `resource` block with every property explicit. No defaults, full fidelity to the underlying schema.

L2 Constructs (`Bucket`, `Function`) are what Bicep modules aspire to be: opinionated wrappers that apply secure defaults (server-side encryption on, public access blocked) and expose ergonomic APIs. Where Bicep forces you to manually author `roleAssignment` resources and wire `dependsOn`, L2s internalize IAM through grant methods like `grantRead()` — the dependency graph is implicit.

L3 Constructs (`ApplicationLoadBalancedFargateService`) are pattern libraries — equivalent to a Bicep module registry entry that provisions an entire architecture: VPC, ECS cluster, ALB, and target group in a single instantiation.

A Stack is CDK's deployment unit, mirroring a Bicep file paired with `az deployment group create`. The key difference: stacks are composable in code — pass cross-stack outputs as constructor props instead of chaining parameter files.

bytes.topic: Drift Detection — what happens when infra is changed manually, reconciliation
bytes.title: Bicep Drift Detection — What-If, Complete Mode, and Reconciliation
bytes.body:
Bicep deployments default to `--mode Incremental`, so ARM only touches resources declared in your template — manual portal or CLI changes accumulate silently. This is drift: live state diverging from your `.bicep` source of truth.

Run `az deployment group what-if` to surface it. This invokes ARM's `/whatIf` endpoint, diffing your compiled ARM JSON against the live resource graph. Output is categorized as `Create`, `Modify`, `Delete`, or `NoChange` — no resources are touched during this phase.

To reconcile, redeploy with `--mode Complete`. ARM treats your Bicep template as the sole authority: any resource in the resource group absent from the template is deleted. Scope it tightly — use `targetScope = 'resourceGroup'` in your Bicep file and pin `--resource-group` explicitly.

For stricter drift prevention, `az stack group create` (Azure Deployment Stacks) adds deny assignments that block out-of-band changes at the ARM layer before drift can occur. Stacks also expose `--action-on-unmanage deleteAll` to automate cleanup of orphaned resources on teardown, giving you Terraform-style lifecycle control natively in Bicep.
bytes.code_snippet:
# 1. Detect drift — no resources are modified
az deployment group what-if \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @params.json

# 2. Reconcile — Complete mode deletes any resource
#    in myRG that is NOT declared in main.bicep
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters @params.json \
  --mode Complete

# 3. Prevent drift at the source with Deployment Stacks
az stack group create \
  --name myStack \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --action-on-unmanage deleteAll

bytes.topic: Secrets in IaC — never store secrets in state, vault/secrets manager integration
bytes.title: Bicep Secrets — Keep Them Out of ARM Deployment History
bytes.body: Every value you pass as a plain `string` parameter in Bicep lands in Azure's ARM deployment history — readable by anyone with `Microsoft.Resources/deployments/read`. For secrets, use the `@secure()` decorator: ARM redacts those values from history and logs. But the real pattern is never accepting the secret in Bicep at all. Reference Key Vault directly via a `getSecret()` call on the Key Vault `existing` resource — the secret is resolved server-side during deployment and never touches your parameter file or deployment history. Pair this with a `Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults` `existing` reference scoped to the correct resource group. For dynamic secrets like connection strings generated post-deploy (e.g. a storage account key), use a `listKeys()` call and pipe it straight into the consuming resource's `properties` block — again, never materializing the value as a named output. Mark any output that must carry a secret with `@secure()` too, or ARM will log it. The combination of `@secure()` + Key Vault references + avoiding secret outputs is the full defense.

bytes.topic: Testing IaC — terratest, checkov, tfsec, policy as code
bytes.title: Bicep IaC Testing — Checkov, PSRule, and Native Test Framework in CI
bytes.body:
Bicep's native test framework (`test` keyword, Bicep ≥ 0.21) lets you validate module parameters and output shapes without deploying. `bicep test ./storage.test.bicep` compiles and evaluates `test` blocks, catching type mismatches and `assert` violations at build time — before any ARM call fires.

For static security scanning, Checkov has first-class Bicep support: `checkov --framework bicep -d .` evaluates `.bicep` source directly against CKV_AZURE rules — open NSG ports, missing `httpsOnly`, unencrypted storage accounts. Unlike tfsec (Terraform-only), Checkov parses Bicep's AST natively.

PSRule for Azure is the policy-as-code layer: `ps-rule-assert --module PSRule.Rules.Azure --input-path .` applies Azure Well-Architected Framework rules against compiled ARM output (`bicep build`) or `what-if` results. Rules like `Azure.Storage.SecureTransfer` and `Azure.AKS.MinNodeCount` fail the pipeline before a resource is touched.

Wire these as an ordered CI gate: `bicep build` → `checkov` → `ps-rule-assert` → `az deployment group what-if`. Each stage fails fast on its own signal — Checkov catches source-level misconfigurations; PSRule enforces org-wide policy compliance; `what-if` gates against unexpected ARM resource diffs.
bytes.code_snippet:
# CI gate — runs before az deployment group create
bicep build main.bicep --outfile main.json

# Static security scan — Bicep AST, no ARM conversion needed
checkov --framework bicep -d . --compact --quiet

# Policy-as-code: Azure Well-Architected Framework rules
ps-rule-assert --input-path . \
  --module PSRule.Rules.Azure \
  --outcome Fail,Error

# What-if preview — validate ARM diff, no deployment
az deployment group what-if \
  --resource-group rg-staging \
  --template-file main.json \
  --parameters @params.staging.json

bytes.topic: Import Existing Resources — bringing existing infra under IaC management
bytes.title: Bicep existing Keyword — Importing Real Infra Into Your IaC
bytes.body: Bicep separates two distinct intents when dealing with pre-existing Azure resources. The `existing` keyword lets you reference a resource Bicep doesn't own — pulling its properties and resource ID without touching its lifecycle. This is ideal for cross-RG dependencies like a shared Key Vault or VNet. To actually bring a resource under Bicep management, define it normally and redeploy — ARM is idempotent and adopts matching resources in-place rather than recreating them. The safest workflow: export the resource as ARM JSON via the Azure Portal, run `az bicep decompile --file exported.json` to get a `.bicep` scaffold, reconcile drift against live config, then deploy with `az deployment group create --what-if` first. Watch for properties ARM silently sets (like `defaultAction` on storage firewall rules) that will show as diffs on first what-if but won't cause downtime. Once deployed, the resource is owned by your Bicep module — subsequent changes flow through code. Never mix `existing` references and full resource declarations for the same resource in one template; ARM will conflict on the resource ID.

bytes.topic: Destroy Safety — preventing accidental destruction, lifecycle rules
bytes.title: Bicep Destroy Safety — Lifecycle Rules to Prevent Accidental Resource Deletion
bytes.body:
Bicep's `existing` keyword and the `@sealed()` decorator protect against drift, but the real guard against accidental deletion is the resource `lock` resource type and the `resourceLock` resource provider. Deploy a `Microsoft.Authorization/locks` child resource with `level: 'CanNotDelete'` to block deletions even from contributors with full RBAC. For stateful resources — Storage Accounts, Key Vaults, PostgreSQL Flexible Servers — this is non-negotiable.

Beyond locks, use `dependsOn` deliberately: if a database lock depends on the database resource, Bicep tears down the lock before the DB during `az deployment group delete`, which is the correct destruction order. Pair this with `az deployment group what-if --mode Complete` before any Complete-mode deploy — Complete mode deletes resources absent from the template, so what-if is your last line of defense. Set `'Complete'` mode only in pipelines behind a manual approval gate. For incremental day-to-day deploys, omit `--mode` entirely to default to `Incremental`.

bytes.topic: Cost Estimation — infracost, estimating before apply
bytes.title: Bicep Cost Estimation — Shift Left with Infracost Before az deployment create
bytes.body:
Infracost doesn't natively parse `.bicep` files — it consumes ARM JSON. The workflow is: compile first, estimate second. Run `az bicep build --file main.bicep --outfile main.arm.json`, then point Infracost at the output. Infracost reads resource types and SKUs from the ARM template's `resources` array, maps them to Azure Pricing API entries, and surfaces a cost breakdown before any `az deployment group create` ever runs.

In CI, wire this into your PR pipeline: compile the Bicep, run `infracost diff --path main.arm.json --compare-to base.arm.json`, and post the delta as a PR comment. A `Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines` switching from `Standard_B2s` to `Standard_D4s_v3` will surface a ~4× monthly cost increase before merge.

Watch for resources Infracost can't price — `Microsoft.Resources/deploymentScripts` and custom roleAssignments show as `$0`. Treat those as manual estimation items. For modules using `existing` keyword references, costs won't appear — only net-new resources declared with `resource` blocks are evaluated.
bytes.code_snippet:
# 1. Compile Bicep to ARM JSON
az bicep build --file infra/main.bicep --outfile infra/main.arm.json

# 2. Estimate costs before deployment
infracost breakdown --path infra/main.arm.json

# 3. In CI: diff against base branch ARM output
infracost diff \
  --path infra/main.arm.json \
  --compare-to infra/base.arm.json \
  --format json \
  --out-file infracost-diff.json

# 4. Post to PR (GitHub Actions)
infracost comment github \
  --path infracost-diff.json \
  --repo $GITHUB_REPOSITORY \
  --pull-request $PR_NUMBER \
  --github-token $GITHUB_TOKEN

bytes.topic: CI/CD Integration — automated plan on PR, apply on merge, drift alerts
bytes.title: Bicep CI/CD — Automated Plan on PR, Apply on Merge, Drift Alerts
bytes.body:
Wire Bicep into GitHub Actions with three distinct pipeline stages: validate/what-if on pull requests, apply on merge to main, and scheduled drift detection.

On PR, run `az deployment sub what-if` with `--template-file main.bicep` and post results as a PR comment using the `azure/CLI` action. This gives reviewers a diff of resource changes before merge — equivalent to Terraform's plan output, but native to ARM.

On merge, trigger `az deployment sub create` with `--confirm-with-what-if false` for a clean apply. Scope your deployment to the correct level (`sub`, `group`, `tenant`, or `mg`) matching your Bicep `targetScope`.

For drift, schedule a nightly workflow that runs `what-if` against your live environment and fails if `changeType` includes `Modify` or `Delete` in the JSON output (`--output json`). Pipe through `jq` to filter non-`NoChange` entries and alert via a GitHub issue or Teams webhook.

Always pin your `az bicep` CLI version in the workflow to avoid silent template compilation differences across runs.
bytes.code_snippet:
- name: Bicep What-If on PR
  uses: azure/CLI@v2
  with:
    azcliversion: 2.58.0
    inlineScript: |
      az deployment sub what-if \
        --location eastus \
        --template-file infra/main.bicep \
        --parameters infra/main.bicepparam \
        --output json > whatif.json
      cat whatif.json | jq '.changes[] | select(.changeType != "NoChange")'

- name: Bicep Apply on Merge
  if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
  uses: azure/CLI@v2
  with:
    azcliversion: 2.58.0
    inlineScript: |
      az deployment sub create \
        --location eastus \
        --template-file infra/main.bicep \
        --parameters infra/main.bicepparam

bytes.topic: Linting & Formatting — tflint, terraform fmt, consistent style
bytes.title: Bicep Linting & Formatting — Enforcing Consistent Style with bicepconfig.json
bytes.body: Bicep ships a built-in linter — no tflint needed. Rules are controlled via `bicepconfig.json` at the repo root, which Bicep CLI picks up automatically during `bicep build` and in the VS Code extension. Each rule has a `level` of `off`, `warning`, or `error`. Elevate `no-unused-params`, `no-unused-vars`, and `secure-secrets-in-params` to `error` in CI to block merges on common hygiene issues. The `prefer-interpolation` rule catches string concatenation that should use `'${var}'` syntax. For formatting, `bicep format main.bicep` (CLI v0.14+) rewrites indentation, spacing, and newlines in place — pipe it into a pre-commit hook or a GitHub Actions step with `bicep format --check` to fail on unformatted files without mutating them. Combining linter-as-error with format-check in CI means style drift never reaches main. Run `az bicep upgrade` to keep the linter rule set current; Microsoft adds new rules with each release.
